description: Explains how Requests exposes its public developer API through top-level helpers, Session-backed execution, package exports, and documented exception classes.
document:
Requests presents its public interface through the developer API documentation and the package namespace imported by applications. The documentation in docs/api.rst frames this as the place for all developer-facing interfaces, with the main interface centered on simple request helpers that return Response objects. It then points readers to the stateful session object, lower-level request and response classes, authentication helpers, cookie utilities, status code lookup, and the documented exception types. For easy usage, the important idea is that the public API is intentionally shallow: callers can start with simple functions, then move to a session when they need persistence or configuration.

The top-level implementation in src/requests/api.py is deliberately thin. `request` is the generic helper that constructs a temporary session as a context manager and delegates the real work to the session request path. The context manager matters because it closes the session after the call, avoiding open sockets that can otherwise look like leaks or trigger resource warnings. The method-specific helpers normalize common call shapes and forward the same options onward; `head` is the notable special case because it disables redirects by default unless the caller provides an explicit redirect setting. The posting helper exposes body data and JSON input directly, while the other verb helpers rely on forwarded keyword options for less common settings.

The reusable counterpart lives in src/requests/sessions.py. `Session` is the public object for cookie persistence, connection pooling, and shared configuration across requests. Its initialization sets up sensible defaults for user metadata, transport behavior, TLS verification, environment trust, cookies, redirect limits, and mounted adapters for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. It also supports context-manager use, where exiting closes every adapter. This gives the public interface two modes with the same response contract: short-lived top-level calls for simple one-off requests, and a long-lived session for repeated calls with shared state.

A session request first creates a Request model, prepares it with session defaults, merges environment-derived settings, and sends the prepared form. The preparation step layers request-specific values over session defaults, including cookies and the metadata that affects headers, authentication, query strings, and response hooks. If environment trust is enabled and no authentication was supplied directly or on the session, netrc credentials may be used. Environment merging can also add proxy settings and certificate bundle paths from supported environment variables before the request is sent.

The sending path is where the public session methods meet the transport layer. `send` requires a prepared request rather than a raw request object, chooses an adapter by URL prefix, times the adapter call, dispatches response hooks, stores cookies back onto the session, resolves redirects when allowed, and forces response content to be downloaded unless streaming is active. When redirects are not followed, it records the next prepared redirect request so the response can still expose follow-up behavior. Adapter mounting is public as well: longer URL prefixes are sorted before shorter ones so more specific adapters win.

The package initializer, src/requests/__init__.py, makes these pieces available from import requests. It re-exports the request helpers, the session class and compatibility constructor, request and response model classes, common exception classes, and status codes. It also performs import-time dependency checks, configures urllib3-related warning behavior, installs a null logging handler, and leaves file mode warnings visible by default. Those setup details support the public API by making the namespace convenient while guarding common dependency and logging edge cases.

The exception hierarchy in src/requests/exceptions.py gives callers stable public failure types. `RequestException` is the base class for request failures and can carry the response and request involved, deriving the request from the response when possible. The documented exceptions cover HTTP errors, connection failures, too many redirects, connect and read timeouts, general timeout handling, and JSON decoding failures. The timeout base is useful because it catches both connect and read timeout subclasses, while the JSON decode class preserves compatibility with the underlying JSON library error behavior and still belongs to the Requests exception family.
